speaker: interviewer
text: --five. And this interview is with Esther Rudomin Hautzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my friend, Heniek Cender, in Lodz, Poland, in 1946, after the war. And as I had said before in our interview, he survived Theresienstadt and shot himself dead in January or February of 1947, for a reason no one could ever fathom nor explain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This is a picture taken of my parents' 25th wedding anniversary party, a surprise party, which Walter and I gave. I am on-- the first on the left. My father is next to me. Next to him is my mother. And behind my mother is my husband, Walter Hautzig. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father and me on his 90th birthday, in New York City, in-- on-- in May of 1989, six months before he died-- full of pep, full of good cheer, full of jokes, full of everything being wonderful, wonderful. I hope it's wonderful, wonderful in heaven for him, too. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of David, my son, David, who is now 30. This was taken in January, 1995, in New Orleans, after a concert, which--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in New Orleans after a concert which Walter played with the New Orleans Symphony. And David, and-- and Debbie, and Willie went down to hear. And I stayed in New York. And I think it's one of the nicest pictures of the two men-- our family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of David at his bar mitzvah, in Jerusalem, in 1979-- no-- 1978. He had his bar mitzvah by the Western Wall. It rained, and he had to have it inside rather than outside. And he is reading a portion of the Torah. And the figure next to him is his Hebrew teacher, Jeff Smith, who moved to Israel and lived in northern Israel, and came down to Jerusalem to officiate at David's bar mitzvah. I said when he was born-- to my mother-- that we are not going to have chopped liver sculptures when David is bar mitzvah. We are going to go to Jerusalem. And in 1965, that was not a common thing to have planned. But we planned it, and we did it. And it was the highlight of many years of our lives. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of our daughter, Deborah Hautzig, and her husband, William Devries. Each of them has kept their name. And they're on vacation. And Willie is the dearest, kindest, most sensible member of our family. And we are very lucky to have him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Too loud, Walter. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph I took in Vilna, in July 1993, during the trip I described in the interview before. One of the great tragedies, if one can call that a tragedy, was that I put in the same film twice in the camera. And after crying about it for months-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --after I took the pictures, I decided I will stop crying and make them meaningful. This is a picture of the [NON-ENGLISH], the memorial service at Ponar. In the foreground are people from Israel who are filming the people in the middle, who stand at Ponar's monument. However, over them is a picture of me in the old city, which was on an entirely different print. The remarkable thing about it to me is that it wasn't wasted, because the people who are honoring those who died are in the physical place of where the Vilna ghetto was during World War II. And I am somehow there in the middle, looking at the spirits of people who are gone and whom I loved, and the people who've come to honor their memories. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is another superimposed picture. The background is one of the oldest courtyards in the Jewish section of Vilna, which was part of the ghetto. It is one of the few courtyards that has remained whole until 1930-- 1993. The people on the ground are two hazonim, two cantors, who came from America for the ceremonies of the 50th yahrtzeit, 50th anniversary, of the destruction of the Vilna ghetto, and other people in Ponar. And the man in the foreground on the right is an Israeli who is observing the memorial service. And the one picture superimposed on the other is almost like putting another generation of Jews in the courtyard where Jews lived, and all of whom died. And we came to honor their spirits. And I don't know. God's hand must have-- must have guided my camera, or else my mother wanted to make sure that my memories will remain intact and that I will have no clear picture of Vilna as it was before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty-- your from out of view is pretty.
